questions.question: Explain the fundamental difference between protection and security in an operating system.
keyPoints: Protection refers to the internal mechanisms that control the access of processes and users to the resources defined by the computer system.
Security is a broader term that defends the system's resources from external threats, such as unauthorized access from external users or malware.
Protection is an internal problem, while security requires considering the external environment outside the system.

questions.question: Describe the principle of least privilege and explain how it enhances system security. Provide a concrete example of its implementation.
keyPoints: The principle of least privilege dictates that a process or user should be given only those privileges essential to performing its intended task.
It enhances security by minimizing the potential damage from an attack or an accidental error, as the compromised entity has limited access.
An example is a user account created for everyday tasks that does not have administrative (root/superuser) privileges, preventing system-wide changes.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Access Control List (ACL) and Capability List models for implementing access control. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each approach.
keyPoints: An ACL is an object-centric (or resource-centric) model: it is a list attached to an object specifying which subjects have access and what operations they can perform.
A Capability List is a subject-centric model: it is a list of keys (capabilities) held by a subject, each granting access to a specific object and defining permitted operations.
Advantage of ACL: Easy to review and change all permissions for a specific object. Disadvantage: Harder to determine all objects a subject can access.
Advantage of Capabilities: Efficient for a process to present a capability for access. Disadvantage: Capabilities can be difficult to revoke and manage system-wide.
